moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Bird Opening, an offbeat but principled choice that immediately challenges Black's control of the center. By pushing f4, we control the critical e5 square and prepare to support a potential e4 advance, while keeping our position flexible and less theoretical than 1.e4 or 1.d4. This move has appealed to creative players like Larsen and Radjabov because it sidesteps heavy preparation while maintaining strategic depth.

Black strikes at our center immediately, taking advantage of the fact that we haven't yet controlled e5 with a pawn. This aggressive response forces us to make an important decision—we can either defend the e5 pawn or capture it and accept complications. This is the critical moment where the opening's character will be determined.

We capture the pawn, entering the Lasker Gambit territory where we've sacrificed the f-pawn for central control and open lines. By recapturing on e5, we establish a pawn on a strong central square and open the f-file for potential piece activity, accepting that Black will have dynamic counterplay in exchange for our spatial advantage.

Black immediately attacks our e5 pawn and begins counterplay against our center, which is the key idea in the Lasker Gambit. By playing f6, Black prepares to play d5 next (striking at the center) and keeps our position under pressure, turning the gambit into a sharp tactical battle where both sides have concrete plans rather than a slow positional squeeze.
